perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach (or threatened breach) of this Agreement by the Receiving Party or its Representatives and that the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance, as a remedy for any such breach (or threatened breach), without proof of damages, and each party further agrees to waive, and use its best efforts to cause its Representatives to waive any requirement for the securing or posting of any bond in connection with any such remedy. Such remedies shall not be the exclusive remedies for a breach of this Agreement, but will be in addition to all other remedies available at law or in equity.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach (or threatened breach) of this Agreement by the Receiving Party or its Representatives and that the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance, as a remedy for any such breach (or threatened breach). Such remedies shall not be the exclusive remedies for a breach of this Agreement, but will be in addition to all other remedies available at law or in equity.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the waiver of a bond requirement for equitable relief could violate Delaware Court of Chancery Rule 65.1, which governs security requirements for preliminary injunctions and temporary restraining orders. Although the rule allows for waivers, deleting the clause removes an explicit acknowledgement of the waiver.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the waiver of bond requirements, the agreement becomes ambiguous regarding compliance with Delaware Court of Chancery Rule 65.1, potentially leading to legal challenges if a party seeks an injunction. Delaware courts generally require a bond, so explicit waiver is essential.

perturbation.justification: The original text includes a waiver of any bond requirement for equitable relief, while the changed text removes this waiver. According to the perturbation and law explanations, this omission creates ambiguity and potential non-compliance with Delaware Court of Chancery Rule 65.1, which generally requires a bond unless explicitly waived. The scraped snippets do not directly confirm or deny the information in the explanation. However, the removal of an explicit waiver could be interpreted as a contradiction of standard legal procedures, as it opens the door for a court to require a bond, potentially hindering the Disclosing Party's ability to obtain equitable relief.

perturbation.original_text: To explore the Transaction, it may be necessary for each party to disclose certain Confidential Information to the other party. “ Confidential Information ” shall mean any information disclosed or provided to one party (the “ Receiving Party ”) on or after the Effective Date by or on behalf of the other party (the “ Disclosing Party ”), which the Disclosing Party has not released publicly and which the Disclosing Party considers confidential and/or in which the Disclosing Party has a proprietary interest. Confidential Information includes, without limitation, information, know-how, specifications, materials, models, plans, discoveries, trade secrets (as such term is defined in the Uniform Trade Secrets Act in effect on the Effective Date), records, data, business, marketing, manufacturing and financial records, operations and strategies, invention plans, distribution channels, and technical and product information, customer data, product services, information of the Disclosing Party’s subsidiaries and entities under its control and other communications concerning the Transaction and/or the Disclosing Party’s business and operations, together with all portions of analyses, compilations, notes, studies and other documents prepared by or for the benefit of the Receiving Party which contain or otherwise reflect any of the foregoing.
perturbation.changed_text: To explore the Transaction, it may be necessary for each party to disclose certain Confidential Information to the other party. “ Confidential Information ” shall mean any information disclosed or provided to one party (the “ Receiving Party ”) on or after the Effective Date by or on behalf of the other party (the “ Disclosing Party ”), which the Disclosing Party has not released publicly and which the Disclosing Party considers confidential and/or in which the Disclosing Party has a proprietary interest. Confidential Information includes, without limitation, information, know-how, specifications, materials, models, plans, discoveries, records, data, business, marketing, manufacturing and financial records, operations and strategies, invention plans, distribution channels, and technical and product information, customer data, product services, information of the Disclosing Party’s subsidiaries and entities under its control and other communications concerning the Transaction and/or the Disclosing Party’s business and operations, together with all portions of analyses, compilations, notes, studies and other documents prepared by or for the benefit of the Receiving Party which contain or otherwise reflect any of the foregoing.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'trade secrets (as such term is defined in the Uniform Trade Secrets Act in effect on the Effective Date)' from the definition of Confidential Information, the contract fails to explicitly acknowledge and protect trade secrets as defined by law, potentially contradicting the Delaware Uniform Trade Secrets Act (DUTSA).
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware's DUTSA provides a legal framework for protecting trade secrets. By omitting the explicit reference to trade secrets and the definition provided by DUTSA, the agreement weakens the protection afforded to such information, creating potential conflict with DUTSA's provisions for injunctive relief and damages for misappropriation.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the explicit reference to 'trade secrets' and its definition under the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, which introduces a legal contradiction. The original text acknowledges trade secrets are defined under the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'trade secret' and details 'misappropriation' of trade secrets as acquisition by improper means or disclosure/use without consent. By omitting this reference, the contract weakens the protection of trade secrets and could conflict with DUTSA's provisions.

perturbation.original_text: Unless and until a Definitive Agreement (as defined below) concerning the Transaction has been executed, neither party nor its affiliates nor its or its affiliates’ Representatives shall have any legal obligation to the other party of any kind whatsoever with respect to the Transaction, whether by virtue of this Agreement (except as expressly provided herein), any other written or oral expression with respect to the Transaction, or otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless and until a Definitive Agreement (as defined below) concerning the Transaction has been executed, neither party nor its affiliates nor its or its affiliates’ Representatives shall have any legal obligation to the other party of any kind whatsoever with respect to the Transaction.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'whether by virtue of this Agreement (except as expressly provided herein), any other written or oral expression with respect to the Transaction, or otherwise' suggests that obligations could arise *outside* the definitive agreement, contradicting the overall intention of limiting liability prior to a final agreement. While Delaware law allows parties to limit pre-contractual liability, removing this clause creates ambiguity and could lead to claims of detrimental reliance or promissory estoppel.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of Contract Law - Promissory Estoppel in Delaware
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a statute, the doctrine of promissory estoppel, as adopted by Delaware courts and reflected in the Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 90, allows for enforcement of promises in the absence of a formal contract if certain conditions are met (detrimental reliance). By removing the clause explicitly negating pre-definitive agreement obligations based on other communications, the contract potentially opens itself up to promissory estoppel claims, contradicting the intended limitation of liability.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: promissory estoppel | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you promissory estoppel Overview Within contract law , promissory estoppel refers to the doctrine that a party may recover on the basis of a promise made when the party's reliance on that promise was reasonable , and the party attempting to recover detrimentally relied on the promise. Recognition In Cohen v. Cowles Media Co . 501 US 663 (1991) , the Supreme Court recognized promissory estoppel as a "state law doctrine creating legal obligations never explicitly assumed by the parties that are enforceable." Consequences An agreement made by promissory estoppel will typically have the same binding effects on parties that a valid contract would. If a party breaches an obligation created by promissory estoppel, a court can choose to assign either reliance damages or expectation damages . Further Reading For more on promissory estoppel, see this Fordham Law Review article , this UCLA Law Review article , and this University of Chicago Law Review article . Keywords contract contract law promissory estoppel ESTOPPEL BILATERAL CONTRACTS Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modification omits a crucial clause that negates pre-definitive agreement obligations, potentially exposing the parties to promissory estoppel claims. This omission creates a legal contradiction because, as the Law Explanation states and Scraped Law Snippet 1 confirms, promissory estoppel allows for the enforcement of promises even without a formal contract if there is detrimental reliance. By removing the language that explicitly excludes obligations outside the definitive agreement, the changed text contradicts the original intention of limiting liability and opens the door to claims based on pre-contractual communications or conduct.
